content.text:
The `include` array doesn't cover `*.mdx` files — but there's a bigger problem with my fix. Adding a top-level `import` to `mdx.d.ts` turns it from an ambient declaration file into a module, which breaks the `declare module '*.mdx'` wildcard. That's likely what's causing the "cannot find module" errors now (TypeScript can no longer see the declaration as ambient).

The fix is to use an inline import type instead of a top-level import:

result.output: The file /Volumes/Development/next-www-toastboy/mdx.d.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

input.description: Run typecheck to verify the fix

result.output:
> typecheck
> tsc --noEmit

content.text: Clean. The inline `import('react').JSX.Element` references React without a top-level import, so the file stays ambient and the `declare module '*.mdx'` wildcard remains globally visible.

These component overrides drop most incoming props (e.g., id, className, aria-*, event handlers). This can break heading deep-links (IDs added by remark/rehype plugins), code block language classes (language-*), and other accessibility attributes. Accept and forward ...props (including id/className) to the Mantine components, and consider handling pre separately for fenced code blocks so multi-line code and syntax highlighting aren’t degraded.
Let me look at the current file before editing:

result.output: The file /Volumes/Development/next-www-toastboy/mdx-components.tsx has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Two issues to fix: the ternary operator placement (ESLint wants `?` and `:` at end of line) and the unsafe `children` argument (typed as `any` from MDX's index signature — annotating the parameter fixes it).

result.output: The file /Volumes/Development/next-www-toastboy/mdx-components.tsx has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Clean. The explicit `React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLPreElement>` annotation gives `children` the type `React.ReactNode` (not `any`), satisfying the unsafe-argument rule, and the ternary operators are now at end-of-line.

The pre renderer drops all incoming <pre> props (e.g. data-*, id, style, and sometimes className coming from MDX/rehype pipelines). Consider accepting ...props and forwarding the relevant attributes onto the rendered element (or explicitly documenting that they are intentionally ignored) to avoid surprising behavior and improve interoperability with MDX plugins.

content.text: Clean. The `pre` handler now destructures `className` separately to merge it with the inner `code` element's `language-*` class (both sources matter — rehype plugins can annotate either element), and forwards all remaining `pre` props (`id`, `style`, `data-*`, `aria-*`, etc.) to `Code block` via `...preProps`.

@mdx-js/loader (and often @next/mdx) are build-time dependencies in Next.js setups, not runtime dependencies. Consider moving build-only MDX packages to devDependencies (keeping @mdx-js/react if it’s required at runtime) to reduce production install surface area and make dependency intent clearer.

content.text:
Done. All three packages moved to `devDependencies`:

| Package | Reason |
|---|---|
| `@next/mdx` | webpack plugin, only used in `next.config.mjs` at build time |
| `@mdx-js/loader` | webpack loader, invoked by `@next/mdx` during compilation |
| `@mdx-js/react` | peer dep of `@next/mdx`; not imported anywhere in runtime code |

When rel already contains noopener/noreferrer, this logic can produce duplicates in the computed rel string. Consider normalizing via a set-based merge (split on whitespace, add required tokens, then re-join) to keep the output stable and easier to reason about.

content.text: Clean. The `Set` deduplicates any tokens already present in `rel` before joining, so `rel="noopener noreferrer"` on a `_blank` link no longer produces duplicates.

MDX rendering behavior is highly sensitive to MDX/rehype/remark output shape. Add an integration test (or snapshot test) that renders a representative .mdx document containing: (1) an external link with target=\"_blank\" and verifies rel includes noopener noreferrer, and (2) a fenced code block with a language class and verifies the language class and code content are preserved. This guards against regressions when updating Next/MDX plugins.
Let me look at the existing test configs and a sample component test to understand the setup:

content.text:
All 8 tests pass, typecheck is clean, lint is clean. Here's a summary of what was done:

**Test file** (`mdx-components.vitest.spec.tsx`):
- `useTestMDXComponents` — a custom hook bridging Next.js's `(components) => MDXComponents` convention to `@mdx-js/mdx`'s `() => MDXComponents` type, satisfying both TypeScript and `rules-of-hooks`
- `renderMDX` — drives the full `evaluate` pipeline so tests catch regressions in MDX/rehype plugin output shape
- `AnchorWrapper` — a React component that calls `useMDXComponents` (hook-safe, uppercase name), used to render the `a` override in isolation since inline JSX `<a>` bypasses component overrides in MDX
- 5 link tests covering `_blank` injection, no-op for other targets, deduplication, extra tokens, and pipeline routing
- 3 code-block tests covering language class preservation, content preservation, and inline code

**Config changes**: `@mdx-js/mdx` added explicitly to `devDependencies`; `vitest.components.config.ts` `include` extended to cover root-level spec files.

Spreading ...components last allows callers to override the a component and potentially remove the noopener/noreferrer hardening for target=\"_blank\". Consider merging in a way that prevents overriding the secure link behavior (e.g., spread components first and then set a, or use a: components.a ?? (...) while keeping the secure default).
The fix is to spread `components` first (so callers can still customise headings, lists, etc.) and place `a` after the spread so it always wins:

The spec now lives at [src/docs/mdx-components.vitest.spec.tsx](src/docs/mdx-components.vitest.spec.tsx), picked up by the scoped `src/docs/**` glob instead of the open-ended root glob. Overriding both pre and code can cause fenced code blocks (<pre><code className=\"language-*\">…) to be processed twice. Depending on MDX’s element mapping order, the pre override may receive a Mantine <Code> child (from the code override) rather than a native <code> element, which can lead to unexpected markup and make it easier to lose language-class/semantics over time. Consider handling fenced blocks exclusively in pre (and making code only style inline code), e.g., have code skip when className indicates a fenced block (language-*) or return a plain code element in that case.

The key insight: `pre` reads `child.props.className` from the *unrendered* React element descriptor — so it works either way — but if `code` renders inline `<Code>` for fenced blocks and `pre` is later changed to pass children through, you'd get double-wrapping. Making `code` return a plain native element for language-tagged content removes that hazard explicitly.

content.text:
18 tests passing, clean. Summary of what changed:

**`mdx-components.tsx`** — `code` now explicitly branches on `language-*` class:
- Language-tagged → returns a native `<code>` element (plain passthrough, no Mantine wrapper)
- No language class → returns Mantine `<Code>` (inline styling)

**`src/docs/mdx-components.vitest.spec.tsx`** — the new test renders the `code` override directly with `className="language-js"` and asserts the result is a native `<code>` tag. This catches any future regression where the fenced-block path accidentally wraps content in a Mantine component again. (The MDX pipeline approach wouldn't work here because `pre` discards the `code` element and never renders it.)

The safeRel construction is quite dense and mixes tokenization, normalization, de-duplication, and formatting in one expression. Extracting this into a small helper (e.g., buildSafeRel(target, rel)) would improve readability and make it easier to add edge-case handling later (like preserving token order or optionally preserving original casing).

The target check is case-sensitive; target=\"_BLANK\" (HTML treats keywords case-insensitively) would bypass the noopener/noreferrer hardening. Consider normalizing before the comparison (e.g., target?.toLowerCase()) so all _blank variants are protected.

buildSafeRel lowercases all existing rel tokens, which is a subtle behavior change for callers supplying rel (even if the semantics are equivalent). If you want to dedupe case-insensitively while preserving original token casing for non-security tokens, consider keeping the original tokens and only using a lowercased set for comparisons.

content.text: 20 tests, clean. The new approach filters out any existing token that case-insensitively matches a required token, then prepends the canonical `noopener`/`noreferrer` — so `NOOPENER` is deduplicated and `Sponsored` is preserved as `Sponsored`. The `Set`-based approach is gone; deduplication of the security tokens is now handled by the filter step rather than by value equality in a Set.
